answer:
Definition

A URL Shortener with Analytics maps short codes to long URLs, tracks clicks (referrer, device, location), and displays analytics on a dashboard. It covers: hashing/encoding for short code generation, Redis caching for fast redirects, and time-series data for analytics charts.

Use-Case — ContactsHub Inspiration

Drawing from Manoj's experience with ContactsHub (CRUD + analytics patterns), a URL shortener applies the same full-stack skills: Fastify API for redirect logic, PostgreSQL/Prisma for URL storage, Redis for cached lookups, React dashboard with Recharts for click analytics.

Code Example

// Fastify URL shortener
import { nanoid } from 'nanoid';

// Create short URL
fastify.post('/api/urls', async (req) => {
  const { originalUrl } = req.body;
  const code = nanoid(7); // 7-char unique code

  const url = await prisma.shortUrl.create({
    data: { code, originalUrl, userId: req.user.id },
  });

  await redis.set(`url:${code}`, originalUrl, 'EX', 86400); // cache 24h
  return { shortUrl: `${BASE_URL}/${code}`, code };
});

// Redirect with analytics tracking
fastify.get('/:code', async (req, reply) => {
  const { code } = req.params;

  // Check cache first
  let originalUrl = await redis.get(`url:${code}`);
  if (!originalUrl) {
    const record = await prisma.shortUrl.findUnique({ where: { code } });
    if (!record) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'Not found' });
    originalUrl = record.originalUrl;
    await redis.set(`url:${code}`, originalUrl, 'EX', 86400);
  }

  // Track click asynchronously
  clickQueue.add({
    code,
    referrer: req.headers.referer || 'direct',
    userAgent: req.headers['user-agent'],
    ip: req.ip,
    timestamp: new Date(),
  });

  return reply.redirect(301, originalUrl);
});

// Analytics endpoint
fastify.get('/api/urls/:code/analytics', async (req) => {
  const clicks = await prisma.click.groupBy({
    by: ['referrer'],
    where: { urlCode: req.params.code },
    _count: true,
    orderBy: { _count: { referrer: 'desc' } },
  });
  return clicks;
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Short code generation: nanoid (7 chars = 3.5 trillion combinations) vs auto-increment (predictable, enumerable).
  • Caching: Redis with 24h TTL for hot URLs; cold URLs fall through to PostgreSQL.
  • Analytics: Async click tracking via job queue — redirect speed isn't slowed by DB writes.
  • Rate limiting: Prevent URL creation spam with per-user rate limits.

Definition

An Interview Scheduling Service manages time slots, detects conflicts, sends calendar invites, and handles timezone conversions. It's a booking system that prevents double-booking interviewers and ensures candidates see available slots in their local timezone.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's HR Leave System (date conflict detection, multi-party approval), an interview scheduler applies the same patterns: date-range overlap queries, multi-entity coordination (interviewer + candidate + room), and real-time notifications when slots change.

Code Example

// Conflict detection query (PostgreSQL + Prisma)
async function findConflicts(interviewerId, startTime, endTime) {
  return prisma.interview.findMany({
    where: {
      interviewerId,
      status: { not: 'cancelled' },
      OR: [
        { startTime: { lt: endTime }, endTime: { gt: startTime } }, // overlap
      ],
    },
  });
}

// Book interview with conflict check
fastify.post('/api/interviews', async (req, reply) => {
  const { interviewerId, candidateId, startTime, endTime, timezone } = req.body;

  // Convert to UTC for storage
  const startUTC = dayjs.tz(startTime, timezone).utc().toDate();
  const endUTC = dayjs.tz(endTime, timezone).utc().toDate();

  // Check conflicts
  const conflicts = await findConflicts(interviewerId, startUTC, endUTC);
  if (conflicts.length > 0) {
    return reply.status(409).send({
      error: 'Time slot conflicts with existing interview',
      conflicts: conflicts.map(c => ({ id: c.id, time: c.startTime })),
    });
  }

  const interview = await prisma.interview.create({
    data: { interviewerId, candidateId, startTime: startUTC, endTime: endUTC, status: 'scheduled' },
  });

  // Send notifications
  await notificationService.send('interview_scheduled', {
    interviewer: interviewerId,
    candidate: candidateId,
    time: startTime,
    timezone,
  });

  return reply.status(201).send(interview);
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Timezone handling: Store all times in UTC, convert to user's timezone in the frontend using dayjs-timezone.
  • Conflict detection: SQL overlap query: startA < endB AND endA > startB.
  • Race conditions: Use database-level unique constraints or SELECT FOR UPDATE to prevent double-booking when two requests arrive simultaneously.
  • Calendar integration: Generate .ics files or integrate with Google Calendar API for automatic calendar invites.

Definition

Collaborative Document Comments let multiple users add comments on specific parts of a document, reply to threads, and see updates in real time. It combines: anchor-based positioning (comment linked to a text range), real-time sync (Socket.io), and threaded conversations.

Use-Case — HR Leave System + ContactsHub Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's experience with Socket.io real-time notifications (HR system) and CRUD patterns (ContactsHub), collaborative comments apply the same skills: WebSocket events for real-time sync, Prisma for comment storage with parent-child threading, and optimistic UI updates.

Code Example

// Comment model (Prisma)
model Comment {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  documentId String
  userId    String
  content   String
  anchor    Json     // { startOffset, endOffset, quotedText }
  parentId  String?  // null = top-level, set = reply
  parent    Comment? @relation("thread", fields: [parentId], references: [id])
  replies   Comment[] @relation("thread")
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}

// API: add comment with real-time broadcast
fastify.post('/api/documents/:docId/comments', async (req) => {
  const comment = await prisma.comment.create({
    data: {
      documentId: req.params.docId,
      userId: req.user.id,
      content: req.body.content,
      anchor: req.body.anchor,
      parentId: req.body.parentId || null,
    },
    include: { replies: true },
  });

  // Broadcast to all users viewing this document
  io.to(`doc:${req.params.docId}`).emit('comment_added', comment);

  return comment;
});

// Client: join document room
socket.emit('join_document', documentId);
socket.on('comment_added', (comment) => {
  setComments(prev => [...prev, comment]);
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Anchoring: Store text offset + quoted text. If document changes, use the quoted text to re-anchor the comment to the nearest match.
  • Threading: Self-referential parentId relation. Top-level comments have parentId: null.
  • Conflict resolution: Optimistic concurrency — if two users reply at the same time, both succeed and appear in chronological order.
  • Notifications: Notify the document author and any users mentioned with @username.

Definition

A Notification Preference Center lets users control which notifications they receive, through which channels (email, push, in-app), and at what frequency. An audit history tracks every preference change for compliance — who changed what, when, and from what value.

Use-Case — HR Leave System Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's HR Leave System (role-based notifications, Socket.io delivery), a preference center extends the notification service with user-configurable settings and a tamper-proof audit log. The same patterns apply: CRUD for preferences, event sourcing for history.

Code Example

// Preference model
model NotificationPreference {
  id        String  @id @default(cuid())
  userId    String
  eventType String  // 'leave_approved', 'leave_submitted', etc.
  email     Boolean @default(true)
  push      Boolean @default(true)
  inApp     Boolean @default(true)
  @@unique([userId, eventType])
}

model AuditLog {
  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId    String
  action    String   // 'preference_updated'
  field     String   // 'email'
  oldValue  String
  newValue  String
  changedBy String   // who made the change
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
}

// Update preference with audit trail
fastify.patch('/api/preferences/:eventType', async (req) => {
  const existing = await prisma.notificationPreference.findUnique({
    where: { userId_eventType: { userId: req.user.id, eventType: req.params.eventType } },
  });

  // Log each changed field
  for (const [field, newValue] of Object.entries(req.body)) {
    if (existing[field] !== newValue) {
      await prisma.auditLog.create({
        data: {
          userId: req.user.id,
          action: 'preference_updated',
          field,
          oldValue: String(existing[field]),
          newValue: String(newValue),
          changedBy: req.user.id,
        },
      });
    }
  }

  return prisma.notificationPreference.update({
    where: { userId_eventType: { userId: req.user.id, eventType: req.params.eventType } },
    data: req.body,
  });
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Default preferences: All channels enabled by default. Users opt-out, not opt-in.
  • Audit immutability: Audit log entries are insert-only — never updated or deleted.
  • Preference check: Notification service reads preferences before routing to any channel.
  • Admin override: Critical notifications (security alerts, password reset) bypass preferences — always delivered.

Definition

A Role-Based Admin Panel with policy-driven access goes beyond simple RBAC by defining permissions as policies — rules that consider context (resource ownership, time, environment) beyond just the user's role. It supports fine-grained access like "managers can only approve leaves for their direct reports."

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

Manoj's HR Leave System uses this pattern directly: the admin panel lets HR configure roles (employee, manager, admin), assign permissions (view, create, approve, delete), and define policies ("managers can only see their team's data"). The 3-level approval chain is itself a policy: leave > 3 days requires VP approval.

Code Example

// Policy engine
const policies = {
  'leave:approve': (user, resource) => {
    if (user.role === 'admin') return true;
    if (user.role === 'manager') {
      return resource.employeeTeamId === user.teamId  // own team only
        && resource.employeeId !== user.id;           // can't self-approve
    }
    return false;
  },
  'leave:view': (user, resource) => {
    if (user.role === 'admin') return true;
    if (user.role === 'manager') return resource.employeeTeamId === user.teamId;
    return resource.employeeId === user.id; // employees see own only
  },
};

function checkPolicy(action, user, resource) {
  const policy = policies[action];
  if (!policy) return false;
  return policy(user, resource);
}

// Middleware
function authorize(action) {
  return async (req, reply, done) => {
    const resource = await getResource(req);
    if (!checkPolicy(action, req.user, resource)) {
      return reply.status(403).send({ error: 'Policy denied' });
    }
    done();
  };
}

fastify.post('/api/leaves/:id/approve',
  { preHandler: [authenticate, authorize('leave:approve')] },
  approveLeave
);

Key Design Decisions

  • Policy vs role: Roles define WHO the user is. Policies define WHAT they can do in context.
  • Centralized engine: All policies in one file — easy to audit and test.
  • Policy testing: Unit test every policy with edge cases (self-approval, cross-team access).
  • Admin panel: Let HR configure policies via UI — stored in database, loaded at startup.

Definition

A Resilient Webhook Ingestion Service receives webhooks from external services, verifies authenticity, processes them reliably, handles retries and duplicates, and provides observability. It's the defensive side of webhook handling — ensuring no event is lost or processed twice.

Use-Case — HR Leave System + ContactsHub Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's webhook handling experience (payroll webhooks in HR system, Cloudinary webhooks in ContactsHub), a resilient ingestion service applies: signature verification, idempotency keys, dead-letter queues, and replay capability for failed events.

Code Example

// Resilient webhook handler
fastify.post('/api/webhooks/:source', async (req, reply) => {
  const { source } = req.params;

  // 1. Verify signature
  if (!verifySignature(source, req.headers, req.body)) {
    return reply.status(401).send();
  }

  // 2. Store raw event (for replay)
  const eventId = req.headers['x-event-id'] || crypto.randomUUID();
  await prisma.webhookEvent.upsert({
    where: { externalId: eventId },
    create: {
      externalId: eventId,
      source,
      payload: req.body,
      status: 'received',
    },
    update: {}, // idempotent — don't overwrite if exists
  });

  // 3. Respond 200 immediately
  reply.status(200).send({ received: true });

  // 4. Queue for processing
  await webhookQueue.add(
    { eventId },
    { attempts: 3, backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 5000 } }
  );
});

// Worker with retry and dead-letter
webhookQueue.process(async (job) => {
  const event = await prisma.webhookEvent.findUnique({
    where: { externalId: job.data.eventId },
  });
  await processEvent(event);
  await prisma.webhookEvent.update({
    where: { id: event.id },
    data: { status: 'processed' },
  });
});

webhookQueue.on('failed', async (job, err) => {
  if (job.attemptsMade >= job.opts.attempts) {
    await prisma.webhookEvent.update({
      where: { externalId: job.data.eventId },
      data: { status: 'dead_letter', error: err.message },
    });
  }
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Store-then-process: Save the raw webhook payload before processing — enables replay for debugging.
  • Idempotency: Upsert by external event ID — duplicate deliveries are silently ignored.
  • Dead-letter queue: Events that fail 3 times move to dead-letter for manual investigation.
  • Admin replay: Endpoint to re-process any event from the store — useful for fixing bugs and replaying lost events.

Definition

A Payments Reconciliation Report Pipeline compares payment records from multiple sources (payment gateway, bank statements, internal ledger), identifies discrepancies, and generates reports. It handles: data ingestion from APIs/files, matching algorithms, exception handling, and scheduled reporting.

Use-Case — CRM Dashboard Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's CRM Dashboard (data aggregation, Recharts visualization) and HR System (scheduled jobs, report generation), a reconciliation pipeline applies: ETL patterns for data ingestion, fuzzy matching for transaction pairing, and dashboard visualization for discrepancy reports.

Code Example

// Reconciliation job (scheduled nightly)
async function reconcilePayments(date) {
  // 1. Ingest from multiple sources
  const [gatewayTxns, bankTxns, ledgerTxns] = await Promise.all([
    fetchGatewayTransactions(date),
    parseBankStatement(date),
    prisma.ledgerEntry.findMany({ where: { date } }),
  ]);

  // 2. Match transactions
  const results = { matched: [], gatewayOnly: [], bankOnly: [], amountMismatch: [] };

  for (const gw of gatewayTxns) {
    const bank = bankTxns.find(b =>
      b.referenceId === gw.transactionId && Math.abs(b.amount - gw.amount) < 0.01
    );

    if (bank) {
      results.matched.push({ gateway: gw, bank });
      bankTxns.splice(bankTxns.indexOf(bank), 1); // remove matched
    } else {
      const partialMatch = bankTxns.find(b => b.referenceId === gw.transactionId);
      if (partialMatch) {
        results.amountMismatch.push({ gateway: gw, bank: partialMatch, diff: gw.amount - partialMatch.amount });
      } else {
        results.gatewayOnly.push(gw);
      }
    }
  }
  results.bankOnly = bankTxns; // unmatched bank transactions

  // 3. Generate report
  await prisma.reconciliationReport.create({
    data: {
      date,
      matchedCount: results.matched.length,
      discrepancyCount: results.gatewayOnly.length + results.bankOnly.length + results.amountMismatch.length,
      details: results,
    },
  });

  // 4. Alert if discrepancies exceed threshold
  if (results.amountMismatch.length > 0) {
    await notifyFinanceTeam(results);
  }
}

Key Design Decisions

  • Multiple source formats: Gateway returns JSON API, bank provides CSV — normalize both to a common schema.
  • Matching strategy: Primary key = transaction reference ID. Secondary = amount + date (fuzzy match).
  • Idempotency: Running reconciliation twice for the same date produces the same report.
  • Alerting: Auto-alert finance team when discrepancies exceed threshold (e.g., > $100 total mismatch).

Definition

A Feature Toggle Platform with Staged Rollout is a self-service system for managing feature flags at scale: create flags, define rollout rules (percentage, user segments, regions), track flag health, and enforce cleanup. It's the internal tooling that powers gradual feature releases.

Use-Case — CRM Dashboard + HR System Patterns

Drawing from Manoj's experience with feature flags in the CRM Dashboard and role-based access in the HR system, a toggle platform combines: percentage-based rollout (consistent hashing by user ID), segment targeting (by role, team, or region), real-time flag evaluation, and an admin dashboard for flag management.

Code Example

// Flag evaluation engine
class FeatureToggleSDK {
  constructor(apiUrl) {
    this.flags = new Map();
    this.fetchFlags(apiUrl);
  }

  async fetchFlags(apiUrl) {
    const response = await fetch(`${apiUrl}/api/flags`);
    const flags = await response.json();
    flags.forEach(f => this.flags.set(f.name, f));
    // Refresh every 30 seconds
    setInterval(() => this.fetchFlags(apiUrl), 30000);
  }

  isEnabled(flagName, context = {}) {
    const flag = this.flags.get(flagName);
    if (!flag || !flag.enabled) return false;

    // Check segment rules
    for (const rule of flag.rules || []) {
      if (rule.type === 'user_id' && rule.values.includes(context.userId)) return true;
      if (rule.type === 'role' && rule.values.includes(context.role)) return true;
    }

    // Percentage rollout (consistent hashing)
    if (flag.rolloutPercent > 0 && context.userId) {
      const hash = hashCode(`${flagName}:${context.userId}`) % 100;
      return hash < flag.rolloutPercent;
    }

    return false;
  }
}

// Admin API: create/update flags
fastify.post('/api/flags', { preHandler: [authenticate, authorize('admin')] }, async (req) => {
  const flag = await prisma.featureFlag.create({
    data: {
      name: req.body.name,
      enabled: false,
      rolloutPercent: 0,
      rules: req.body.rules || [],
      createdBy: req.user.id,
      cleanupDate: dayjs().add(30, 'days').toDate(),
    },
  });
  return flag;
});

// Staged rollout: PATCH to update percentage
fastify.patch('/api/flags/:name', async (req) => {
  await prisma.flagAuditLog.create({
    data: {
      flagName: req.params.name,
      changedBy: req.user.id,
      change: req.body,
    },
  });
  return prisma.featureFlag.update({
    where: { name: req.params.name },
    data: req.body,
  });
});

Key Design Decisions

  • Consistent hashing: Same user always sees the same flag state — no flickering between sessions.
  • Segment rules: Target by user ID (beta testers), role (admins first), or region.
  • Audit log: Every flag change is logged with who, when, and what changed.
  • Cleanup enforcement: Flags have a cleanupDate — admin dashboard highlights overdue flags.
  • Kill switch: Set enabled: false → instantly disables for all users, no deploy needed.
